title: Innovators From Adobe, Blumhouse and More Discuss AI and Democratizing Entertainment at SXSW
content:
BySelome Hailu

AtVarietyNew Frontiers in Entertainment presented by Adobe duringSXSW, innovators across different facets of the entertainment industry came together to discuss the futures they’re working towards.

The first panel, moderated byVariety‘s co-editor-in-chief and co-president Ramin Setoodeh, focused on new technologies and featured Blumhouse’s chief marketing officer Karen Barragan, Mattel’s chief design and innovation officer Chris Down, Adobe’s vice president of generative AI new business ventures Hannah Elsakr, Fox Entertainment’s president of marketing Daren Schillace and the NFL’s senior vice president of social and influencer marketing Ian Trombetta.

Barragan explained that as YouTube grows in cultural dominance, it has helped train a new generation of film directors. “Before, legacy media was a gateholder to who could make a movie. Now you look at someone like Markiplier who made his own movie, self-distributed and still got into 3,000 AMC theaters,” she said. “We have found a couple of directors on YouTube, and they’re making major studio movies.” Blumhouse has produced two movies directed by YouTubers coming out this May: Focus Features’ “Obsession,” directed by Curry Barker; and A24’s “Backrooms,” directed by Kane Parsons. “Where we have found a playground for directors is in a forum where they already have a following. They have an audience. People know them. So the hope is that they’ll migrate and help evolve how the studios have worked before.”

“People are greenlighting themselves, which is an incredible thing,” Elsakr concurred, adding that she believes AI will have a similarly democratizing impact on entertainment. Acknowledging people’s hesitations about AI, she said, “Photoshop, when it launched in the ’90s, people weren’t happy with Adobe. They felt like we were destroying craft. If you talk to the folks at Pixar, when 3D animation came out, that was really jarring for the industry. I think we’re at that [point] that again.”

Schillace spoke about experiencing some trial and error related to implementing AI ethically. “It’s about the transparency of it,” he said. “We’ve put things up on social that we thought were obviously AI, but didn’t say anything about it, and the immediate and vast rejection was surprising. It became about, ‘You’re taking jobs from artists.’ There were artists who actually made this. We didn’t provide any context or the transparency that we should have, and I think there’s so much misinformation out there. Everyone just goes with the headlines of, ‘You’re taking jobs’ — AI is also creating jobs. I understand the balance of that. Even internally, the transparency [is helpful]: ‘We’re now doing this through our operations team. None of you are leaving, but your jobs arethisnow. You incorporate AI into your job. It makes you faster. You can focus.’ The second I say ‘AI,’ people hear ‘headcount.’ I’m like, ‘That’s not what I said.'”

After the tech panel, Setoodeh moderated a fireside chat with actor and producer Mark Duplass, who gave an alternate point of view on AI. Though he emphasized the importance of democratizing the entertainment industry and how widening access to burgeoning filmmaking technology helped launch his career decades ago, he doesn’t have plans to incorporate AI into his creative practice.

AI “plays almost zero role in my work, and it’s not because I am coming from the standpoint of, ‘This is empirically, ethically wrong.’ But I just am such an analog dude that it’s the way that my brain works,” Duplass said. “The way for me to democratize my access to my art and […] make something with what you have available is not to go to AI and build the backgrounds. The way I do that is, ‘Well, I’m just gonna write for two or three actors, and I’m gonna set it in two or three locations, and build it in a way so I can shoot this comfortably in eight days.’ I’ve been sort of AI-ing myself in that regard and using natural, organic tools to do that.”

When asked for his thoughts about the future of indie filmmaking, Duplass said, “You have these things developing: people’s love for vinyl, people’s love for jazz clubs, people’s love for niche things that they want to overpay for to keep alive and nourish. I’m a big proponent and supporter of Vidiots in Los Angeles. And what we’re seeing there is two movie theaters that arefull, and they’re showing movies that are available to stream at home for almost free, and they’re paying 14 bucks to see it in a community with people and talk to people at the bar afterwards. They’re also going into the video store […] to feel like it’s 1997 again. […] Is that affecting the larger economic plays of the industry? No. Might it get to the place where it’s strong enough and niche enough that somebody starts to pay attention and maybe they overpay to get a piece of that company so they can have that good look? We’ve seen things like that. So I’m not a nihilist. I’m a realist, and I have some hope.”
title_zh: 來自Adobe、布倫豪斯(Blumhouse)等公司的創新者在SXSW討論人工智慧與娛樂民主化議題。
content_zh:
由塞洛美·海盧(BySelome Hailu)報導

在由Adobe主辦的《綜藝雜誌》(Variety)新娛樂前沿論壇上，來自娛樂產業不同領域的創新者聚集在一起，討論他們所追求的未來。

首個小組討論由《綜藝雜誌》(Variety)的聯合主編及聯合總裁拉敏·塞圖德(Ramin Setoodeh)主持，重點討論新技術，參與者包括布倫豪斯(Blumhouse)的首席行銷官卡倫·巴拉根(Karen Barragan)、美泰(Mattel)的首席設計及創新官克里斯·唐(Chris Down)、Adobe的生成式人工智慧新業務副總裁漢娜·艾爾薩克(Hannah Elsakr)、福斯娛樂(Fox Entertainment)的行銷總裁達倫·希拉斯(Daren Schillace)以及NFL的社交及影響者行銷高級副總裁伊恩·特朗貝塔(Ian Trombetta)。

巴拉根解釋道，隨著YouTube在文化上的主導地位增強，它幫助培養了一代新的電影導演。她表示：“以前，傳統媒體是誰能拍電影的把關者。現在你看看像馬克·普萊爾(Markiplier)這樣的人，他自己拍了電影，自行發行，還進入了3000家AMC電影院。”她說：“我們在YouTube上發現了一些導演，他們正在拍攝大型製片廠的電影。”布倫豪斯(Blumhouse)將於今年五月推出兩部由YouTuber導演的電影：Focus Features的《迷戀》(Obsession)，由卡瑞·巴克(Curry Barker)執導；以及A24的《後台》(Backrooms)，由凱恩·帕森斯(Kane Parsons)執導。“我們發現導演的遊樂場是在他們已經擁有追隨者的論壇中。他們有觀眾，人們認識他們。所以希望他們能夠轉型並幫助演變製片廠過去的運作方式。”

艾爾薩克(Hannah Elsakr)表示：“人們正在自我批准，這是一件不可思議的事情。”她補充說，她相信人工智慧將對娛樂產業產生類似的民主化影響。她承認人們對人工智慧的猶豫，並表示：“Photoshop在90年代推出時，人們對Adobe並不滿意。他們覺得我們在摧毀工藝。如果你和皮克斯(Pixar)的人談談，當3D動畫出現時，對行業來說確實是非常震撼的。我認為我們又回到了那個[時刻]。”

希拉斯(Daren Schillace)談到了在倫理上實施人工智慧時所經歷的一些試驗和錯誤。他說：“這關乎透明度。”他表示：“我們在社交媒體上發布了一些我們認為顯然是人工智慧的內容，但沒有說明，結果立即遭到廣泛拒絕，這讓人驚訝。這變成了‘你在搶藝術家的工作。’實際上是有藝術家創作了這些。我們沒有提供任何背景或應有的透明度，我認為外面有太多錯誤資訊。每個人都只關注標題‘你在搶工作’——但人工智慧也在創造工作。我理解這之間的平衡。即使在內部，透明度[也是有幫助的]：‘我們現在是通過我們的運營團隊來做這件事。你們都不會離開，但你們的工作現在是這樣的。你們將人工智慧融入到工作中。這會讓你更快，你可以專注。’我一說‘人工智慧’，人們就聽到‘人數’。我說：‘我不是這麼說的。’”

在技術座談會後，塞圖德(Setoodeh)主持了一場與演員兼製片人馬克·杜普拉斯(Mark Duplass)的爐邊談話，他對人工智慧(AI)提出了不同的看法。雖然他強調了民主化娛樂產業的重要性，以及如何擴大對新興電影製作技術的接觸幫助他在幾十年前啟動了自己的職業生涯，但他並不打算將AI納入他的創作實踐中。

杜普拉斯表示：“AI在我的工作中幾乎沒有任何作用，這並不是因為我認為‘這在經驗上和倫理上都是錯誤的’。而是因為我就是這麼一個類比的人，這是我大腦運作的方式。對我來說，民主化我對藝術的接觸和……用你手頭上可用的東西創作的方式，不是去找AI來構建背景。我這樣做的方式是，‘好吧，我就為兩到三位演員寫劇本，並將其設置在兩到三個地點，以便我能在八天內輕鬆拍攝。’在這方面，我已經在用自己的方式進行AI化，並使用自然、有機的工具來做到這一點。”

當被問及對獨立電影未來的看法時，杜普拉斯說：“你會看到這些事物在發展：人們對黑膠唱片的熱愛，人們對爵士樂俱樂部的熱愛，人們對他們願意超支以維持和滋養的小眾事物的熱愛。我是洛杉磯Vidiots的堅定支持者。我們在那裡看到的是兩家影院座無虛席，它們播放的電影幾乎可以在家裡免費串流，而觀眾卻願意花14美元在社區裡與人一起觀看，並在酒吧裡聊天。他們還會進入視頻商店……感覺就像是1997年一樣。……這是否影響了行業的更大經濟運作？不會。可能會發展到某個程度，強大到小眾的地步，以至於有人開始注意，或許他們會超支以獲得該公司的股份，以便能夠擁有那種良好的形象？我們見過這樣的事情。所以我不是虛無主義者。我是現實主義者，我有一些希望。”
